perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of the Agreement each Party shall be, and shall be deemed to be, an independent contractor and not an agent, partner, joint venturer or employee of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of the Agreement each Party shall be, and shall be deemed to be, an independent entity and not an agent, partner, joint venturer or employee of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'independent contractor' to 'independent entity' creates ambiguity. The term 'independent contractor' has specific legal implications, especially concerning liability, taxes, and employment laws. 'Independent entity' is a broader and less defined term, potentially leading to disputes over the intended relationship and responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Internal Revenue Code provides a specific definition of 'employee' and, by implication, distinguishes it from an 'independent contractor'. Replacing the legally defined term with 'independent entity' obfuscates this distinction, creating potential conflicts in tax liability and reporting obligations.

Definitions (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term "wages" means all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include- (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of th
perturbation.justification: The change from "independent contractor" to "independent entity" introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal definition, potentially leading to disputes. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that 'independent contractor' has specific legal implications, while 'independent entity' is broader and less defined. The Law Explanation mentions that replacing the legally defined term with 'independent entity' obfuscates the distinction between employee and independent contractor, creating potential conflicts in tax liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 26 USC 3121, defines "wages" in relation to "employment", implying a defined legal framework around employment status which the modified term could jeopardize.

perturbation.original_text: Qualigen shall maintain appropriate certification status and compliance with the FDA's Quality System Regulation, the Directive of 27 October 1998 on In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices (IVDD) and/or all other applicable regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Qualigen shall maintain appropriate certification status and compliance with the FDA's guidelines and recommendations, the Directive of 27 October 1998 on In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices (IVDD) and/or all other suggested regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Quality System Regulation' with 'FDA's guidelines and recommendations' weakens the obligation. The QSR is a mandatory regulatory requirement (21 CFR Part 820), whereas 'guidelines and recommendations' are non-binding suggestions. Similarly, changing 'applicable regulations' to 'suggested regulations' introduces significant legal ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 820 establishes mandatory quality system requirements for medical device manufacturers. By changing 'Quality System Regulation' to 'FDA's guidelines and recommendations' and 'applicable regulations' to 'suggested regulations', the modified text contradicts the mandatory nature of this regulation, potentially leading to non-compliance and FDA enforcement actions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Home Medical Devices Device Advice: Comprehensive Regulatory Assistance Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP) Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Update: January 31, 2024 The FDA issued the Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR) Final Rule to amend the device current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements of the Quality System (QS) regulation (21 CFR Part 820), incorporating by reference the international standard specific for medical device quality management systems set by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), ISO 13485:2016 Medical devices – Quality management systems – Requirements for regulatory purposes. This final rule is the latest action taken by the FDA to promote consistency in the regulation of devices. This action will harmonize the FDA’s CGMP regulatory framework with that used by other regulatory authorities. The rule is effective February 2, 2026, two years after publication. Until then, manufacturers are required to comply with the QS regulation. Introduction Flexibility of the QS Regulation Applicability of the QS Regulation GMP Exemptions Additional Quality System Information Quality System Regulation and Preamble Introduction Manufacturers must establish and follow quality systems to help ensure that their products consistently meet applicable requirements and specifications. The quality systems for FDA-regulated products (food, drugs, biologics, and devices) are known as current good manufacturing practices (CGMP’s). CGMP requirements for devices in part 820 (21 CFR part 820) were first authorized by section 520(f) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (the Act). Under section 520(f) of the Act, FDA issued a final rule in the Federal Register of July 21, 1978 (43 FR 31 508), prescribing CGMP requirements for medical devices. This regulation became effective on December 18, 1978, and was codified under part 820. In 1990, the FDA undertook the start of the revision of the CGMP regulation to add the design controls authorized by the Safe Medical Devices Act. Also, the agency believed that it would be beneficial to the public and the medical device industry for the CGMP regulation to be consistent, to the extent possible, with the requirements for quality systems contained in applicable international standards, primarily, the International Organization for Standards (ISO) 9001:
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory nature of medical device regulations, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates compliance with the 'FDA's Quality System Regulation,' a legally binding requirement under 21 CFR Part 820, as confirmed by both scraped snippets which refer to 'Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP)' and the 'device current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements of the Quality System (QS) regulation (21 CFR Part 820)'. By changing this to 'FDA's guidelines and recommendations' and 'applicable regulations' to 'suggested regulations', the revised text introduces ambiguity and replaces binding obligations with non-binding suggestions. This directly undermines the enforceability of the quality system requirements and contradicts the explicit regulatory mandate for medical device manufacturers to adhere to CGMP, as stated in the provided law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: Qualigen represents and warrants that no Product delivered by Qualigen under this Agreement will be adulterated or misbranded within the meaning of 21 U.S.C. Sections 351-352, or within the meaning of any other applicable law as such laws are constituted and effective at the time of such shipment or delivery.
perturbation.changed_text: Qualigen represents and warrants that no Product delivered by Qualigen under this Agreement will be considered undesirable or improperly labeled within the general understanding of product quality.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'adulterated or misbranded within the meaning of 21 U.S.C. Sections 351-352' with 'considered undesirable or improperly labeled within the general understanding of product quality' eliminates the reference to specific legal definitions. 'Adulterated' and 'misbranded' have precise meanings under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act). This change creates uncertainty and weakens the protection against substandard products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 351 defines specific conditions under which a drug or device is considered adulterated. By substituting the precise legal definition with a general statement, the modified text contradicts the statutory requirements and potentially allows for the distribution of products that would be deemed illegal under the FD&C Act.

Adulterated drugs and devices A drug or device shall be deemed to be adulterated- (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients; adequate controls in manufacture (1) If it consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, or decomposed substance; or (2)(A) if it has been prepared, packed, or held under insanitary conditions whereby it may have been contaminated with filth, or w
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically refers to 'adulterated or misbranded within the meaning of 21 U.S.C. Sections 351-352', providing a clear legal standard. The changed text replaces this with 'considered undesirable or improperly labeled within the general understanding of product quality', which lacks a specific legal definition and weakens the obligation to comply with the FD&C Act. The law snippet, 21 USC 351, explicitly addresses 'Adulterated drugs and devices' and defines the conditions under which a drug or device is deemed adulterated, thereby contradicting the modified text's vague and non-specific language.
